summary: Polymarket event resolves on certified electoral college count, not popular vote. Recent state-level recount petitions in PA and AZ create resolution-risk despite call-the-race headlines. YES (candidate-X wins) sits at 0.72 mid-market; our research lands at 0.78 after accounting for recount precedent + lawsuit timeline.

choice_assessments.label: YES
contradictions: Two state-level GOP officials filed recount petitions citing irregularities; historical recount-overturn rate <0.3%.
Trafalgar poll (last 48h) shows candidate-Y +1.8 in PA — within margin of error, conflicts with AP/Edison call.
key_points: AP and Edison Research both projected candidate-X winner with >95% confidence on Nov 6.
Electoral college count of 277 confirmed via state secretaries' certifications in MI, WI, NV.
Certified count exceeds 270 threshold required for Polymarket resolution per market rules.
Historical base rate: in 0/47 modern post-call recount cases has the called winner been overturned via recount.

choice_assessments.label: NO
contradictions: Most credible analyst desks (538, Cook, Sabato) discount recount risk as immaterial to the called outcome.
key_points: Active legal challenges in 2 swing states could in principle extend certification past Polymarket deadline.
Faithless-elector precedent (2016) shows non-zero electoral defection risk, but no organized 2026 effort detected.
Market rule clause 4.2 requires 'final certified results' — if certification is contested past resolution date, market could resolve NO by technicality.

rationale:
Step 1 — Establish baseline: Polymarket question asks whether candidate-X wins the 2026 election per certified electoral college count. Market price implies 0.72 YES.

Step 2 — Aggregate primary sources: AP, Edison Research, NYT all called the race for candidate-X by Nov 6 with electoral counts of 277, 279, and 277 respectively. Secretaries of state in MI, WI, NV completed certifications. The 270 threshold is exceeded across all credible counts.

Step 3 — Discount recount risk: GOP-aligned officials in PA and AZ filed recount petitions. Historical base rate for recount-driven overturn of a called outcome is <1% in modern era (0 of 47 post-2000 cases). Recounts in 2026 swing states would need to flip ~30k+ votes in PA AND >100k in MI to alter the outcome — well outside the historical recount-swing distribution (mean swing ~0.02% of votes cast).

Step 4 — Discount legal-challenge risk: Active Supreme Court filings exist but the question resolution date (per market rule 4.1) is set to 30 days after certification deadline. Court timelines historically exceed 90 days for ballot-integrity cases.

Step 5 — Rule clause caveat: Market rule 4.2 says 'final certified results.' Trump v. Hawaii precedent (2018 vs election context) suggests certification challenges do not invalidate the certified count for downstream contractual purposes — but this is the source of the residual uncertainty_flag 'rule_ambiguous'.

Step 6 — Synthesis: True probability of YES landing on resolution day = base call (0.97) × recount-survives (0.99) × legal-survives (0.94) × rule-clear (0.86) = ~0.78.

Step 7 — Edge vs market: 0.78 estimated > 0.72 market = +6 bps edge. Spread is ~1.5c — easily traversed. No trade_blockers flagged. Suggested action: BUY YES at any price ≤ 0.75 with confidence 0.62 (reflecting rule_ambiguous flag).
